# swift-mockable: Complete Documentation

> swift-mockable is a Swift testing library that provides a `@Mockable` macro for generating protocol mocks using compile-time code generation (Swift macros). It produces a `<ProtocolName>Mock` class with call tracking, argument capture, configurable handlers, and property stubs. All generated mocks are wrapped in `#if DEBUG`. MIT licensed.

## Installation

Add the package dependency in `Package.swift`:

```swift
dependencies: [
    .package(url: "https://github.com/yysskk/swift-mockable.git", from: "0.1.0")
]
```

Add `Mockable` to your target:

```swift
.target(
    name: "YourTarget",
    dependencies: ["Mockable"]
)
```

## Quick Start

```swift
import Mockable

@Mockable
protocol UserService {
    func fetchUser(id: Int) async throws -> User
    func saveUser(_ user: User) async throws
    var currentUser: User? { get }
    var isLoggedIn: Bool { get set }
}

let mock = UserServiceMock()

// Configure handlers
mock.fetchUserHandler = { id in
    User(id: id, name: "Test User")
}

// Set properties
mock._currentUser = User(id: 1, name: "Current")
mock.isLoggedIn = true

// Call methods
let user = try await mock.fetchUser(id: 42)

// Verify
#expect(user.id == 42)
#expect(mock.fetchUserCallCount == 1)
#expect(mock.fetchUserCallArgs == [42])

// Reset
mock.resetMock()
#expect(mock.fetchUserCallCount == 0)
```

## What Gets Generated

For each protocol requirement, `@Mockable` generates test-friendly members:

### Functions

- `<method>CallCount` -- number of times the method was called
- `<method>CallArgs` -- array of captured arguments from each call
- `<method>Handler` -- configurable closure that implements the method

### Properties

- `_<property>` -- backing storage for get-only properties (set this in tests)
- `<property>` -- computed property that conforms to the protocol

For get-set properties, the generated property is a stored property, not computed.

### Subscripts

- `subscript<suffix>CallCount` -- number of subscript accesses
- `subscript<suffix>CallArgs` -- array of captured subscript arguments
- `subscript<suffix>Handler` -- configurable closure for get subscripts
- `subscript<suffix>SetHandler` -- configurable closure for set subscripts (get-set only)

### Initializers

- `initCallCount` -- number of times the `init` requirement was invoked
- `initCallArgs` -- array of captured initializer arguments
- Overloaded `init`s append a parameter-type suffix (e.g. `initStringCallCount`)

### Utility

- `resetMock()` -- clears all handlers, call counts, call arguments, and backing properties

## Naming Conventions

### Non-overloaded Methods

For a method named `fetch`, generated members are simply:

- `fetchCallCount`
- `fetchCallArgs`
- `fetchHandler`

### Overloaded Methods

When methods share the same base name, suffixes are appended to disambiguate:

1. Start with sanitized parameter type names.
2. If still ambiguous, append return type (if non-Void), `Async` (for async), and `Throwing` (for throws).

Example:

```swift
func get(url: URL) async -> String
func get(url: URL) async throws -> Data
```

Generates: `getURLStringAsyncHandler` and `getURLDataAsyncThrowingHandler`.

### Subscripts

Subscript-generated names use `subscript<suffix>...`, where the suffix is based on parameter types.

```swift
subscript(index: Int) -> String { get }
```

Generates: `subscriptIntCallCount`, `subscriptIntCallArgs`, `subscriptIntHandler`.

Get-set subscripts also generate `subscript<suffix>SetHandler`.

### Initializers

A sole `init` requirement uses the identifier `init` (`initCallCount`, `initCallArgs`). Overloaded initializers append a parameter-type suffix, matching the method scheme:

```swift
init(host: String)
init(host: String, port: Int)
```

Generates: `initStringCallCount` / `initStringCallArgs` and `initStringIntCallCount` / `initStringIntCallArgs`.

## Supported Features

### Sync, Async, and Throws Methods

All method signatures are supported:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol DataService {
    func loadSync() -> Data
    func loadAsync() async -> Data
    func loadThrowing() throws -> Data
    func loadAsyncThrowing() async throws -> Data
}
```

Each generates its own `CallCount`, `CallArgs`, and `Handler`.

### Properties

Get-only, get-set, and optional properties are supported:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol UserStore {
    var name: String { get }        // generates _name backing storage
    var age: Int { get set }        // generates stored property
    var email: String? { get }      // generates _email optional backing storage
}

let mock = UserStoreMock()
mock._name = "Alice"       // set backing storage for get-only
mock.age = 30              // set stored property directly
mock._email = nil          // optional left as nil
```

### Effectful Read-Only Properties

Read-only properties with `get async`, `get throws`, or `get async throws` are
mocked with a handler and a call counter instead of backing storage (a stored
value cannot model a thrown error):

```swift
@Mockable
protocol TokenProvider {
    var token: String { get async throws }
}

let mock = TokenProviderMock()
mock.tokenHandler = { "secret" }             // or: { throw AuthError.expired }

let token = try await mock.token
// mock.tokenCallCount == 1
```

If the handler is unset, the same defaults as methods apply: Optionals return
`nil`, arrays/sets/dictionaries return empty collections, and any other type
calls `fatalError`.

### Subscripts

Get-only and get-set subscripts:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Cache {
    subscript(key: String) -> Data? { get }
    subscript(index: Int) -> String { get set }
}

let mock = CacheMock()

mock.subscriptStringHandler = { key in
    someData
}

mock.subscriptIntHandler = { index in
    "value"
}

mock.subscriptIntSetHandler = { index, newValue in
    // handle set
}
```

Read-only subscripts with effects (`get async`, `get throws`, `get async throws`)
are supported too. The generated `subscript<suffix>Handler` gains the matching
effects and the accessor is stubbed through it:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol RemoteStore {
    subscript(key: String) -> Int { get async throws }
}

let mock = RemoteStoreMock()
mock.subscriptStringHandler = { key in key.count }   // or: { _ in throw StoreError.missing }

let value = try await mock["swift"]
// mock.subscriptStringCallCount == 1
```

### Typed Throws (SE-0413)

Typed throws (`throws(MyError)`) on methods, effectful properties, and effectful
subscripts is supported. The mock keeps the `throws(MyError)` signature, but the
handler is a plain untyped-throwing closure and the generated body re-throws its
error as the declared type:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Loader {
    func load(id: Int) throws(LoadError) -> String
}
// generates: var loadHandler: (@Sendable (Int) throws -> String)? = nil
//            do { return try _handler(id) } catch { throw error as! LoadError }

let mock = LoaderMock()
mock.loadHandler = { id in throw LoadError() }   // no error-type annotation needed
```

This keeps the package's full deployment range (an untyped handler avoids the
Swift 6 runtime requirement for typed-throws function values) and supports generic
error types (`func run<E: Error>(_ body: () throws(E) -> Void) throws(E)`). If a
handler throws a different error type, the mock traps.

### `@autoclosure` Parameters

`@autoclosure` arguments are evaluated exactly once per call, before the call is
recorded. `CallArgs` and handlers observe the evaluated value, not the closure:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Logger {
    func log(_ message: @autoclosure () -> String)
}

let mock = LoggerMock()
mock.logHandler = { message in }  // handler: (String) -> Void

mock.log("expensive" + " message")
// mock.logCallArgs == ["expensive message"]
```

Notes:

- The argument is evaluated even when no handler is set, so the call can be recorded.
- If evaluating a throwing autoclosure throws, the error propagates before the call
  is recorded (`CallCount` is not incremented).
- An autoclosure's own effects must be covered by the requirement: a throwing
  autoclosure requires a `throws` requirement and an async autoclosure requires an
  `async` requirement; otherwise a compile-time diagnostic is emitted. Effectful
  autoclosures are not supported in subscript requirements.

### Non-Escaping Closure Parameters

A non-escaping closure parameter cannot be stored, so it is excluded from
`CallArgs`. The call is still counted and the closure is still forwarded to the
handler:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Runner {
    func run(label: String, _ body: () -> Void)
}

let mock = RunnerMock()
mock.runHandler = { label, body in body() }   // handler receives the closure

mock.run(label: "task") { print("ran") }
// mock.runCallArgs == ["task"]   (only the storable `label`)
// mock.runCallCount == 1
```

Escaping (`@escaping`), optional, and variadic closures are storable and remain
in `CallArgs` as before.

### `rethrows` Methods

A stored handler cannot satisfy a `rethrows` requirement on its own (a `rethrows`
body may only throw through the requirement's own closure parameters), so the mock
keeps the `rethrows` signature but generates a non-throwing handler that receives
the throwing closures:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Runner {
    func run(_ body: () throws -> Void) rethrows
}

let mock = RunnerMock()
mock.runHandler = { body in try? body() }   // handler: (() throws -> Void) -> Void

try mock.run { /* ... */ }
// mock.runCallCount == 1
```

The handler decides whether to invoke those closures. Because it is non-throwing,
the mock does not itself re-throw their errors; verify behavior through the handler
and the call count.

### Variadic Parameters

Variadic parameters are captured as arrays in `CallArgs`:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Logger {
    func log(_ items: Any...)
}

let mock = LoggerMock()
mock.logHandler = { items in }

mock.log("a", "b", "c")
// mock.logCallArgs contains [[\"a\", \"b\", \"c\"]]
```

### `inout` Parameters

`CallArgs` stores the input snapshot before mutation. Handler return shapes vary:

Single `inout`, no return value -- handler returns the updated value:

```swift
func normalize(_ value: inout Double)
// handler: (Double) -> Double
```

Multiple `inout`, no return value -- handler returns a tuple of updated values:

```swift
func swap(_ a: inout Int, _ b: inout Int)
// handler: (Int, Int) -> (Int, Int)
```

`inout` with return value -- handler returns `(returnValue, inoutArgs)`:

```swift
func removeFirst(_ array: inout [String]) -> String
// handler: ([String]) -> (returnValue: String, inoutArgs: [String])

mock.removeFirstHandler = { array in
    let first = array.first!
    return (returnValue: first, inoutArgs: Array(array.dropFirst()))
}
```

### Generic Methods

Generic type parameters are type-erased to `Any` in `CallArgs` storage and handlers. Generated method implementations cast generic returns back to the requested type:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Repository {
    func find<T>(by id: String) -> T?
}

let mock = RepositoryMock()
mock.findHandler = { id in
    User(id: id, name: "Test") as Any
}

let user: User? = mock.find(by: "123")
```

### Associated Types

Each associated type generates a `typealias` in the mock. If the protocol provides a default type, that type is used; otherwise `Any` is used:

```swift
associatedtype Value = Int   // -> typealias Value = Int
associatedtype Value         // -> typealias Value = Any
```

### Static Methods and Properties

Static methods and properties are supported. They are lock-backed through shared static storage:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Configuration {
    static var shared: String { get }
    static func reset()
}
```

`resetMock()` also resets static generated members.

### Initializer Requirements

A protocol `init` requirement is satisfied by a generated `required init` witness that mirrors the requirement's signature and records the call:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Repository {
    init(configuration: Configuration)
}
// generates:
// var initCallCount: Int = 0
// var initCallArgs: [Configuration] = []
// required init(configuration: Configuration) {
//     initCallCount += 1
//     initCallArgs.append(configuration)
// }

let mock = RepositoryMock(configuration: config)
// mock.initCallCount == 1
// mock.initCallArgs == [config]
```

This unlocks protocols built around the `init(configuration:)` pattern, including code that constructs a conformer generically (`Service(configuration:)` where `Service` is a generic constraint).

Notes:

- Initializers record only -- there is no `initHandler`, because a per-instance handler could never be set before the initializer runs.
- `async`, `throws`, failability (`init?`), and generic clauses are preserved on the witness. A throwing initializer keeps its `throws` signature but never throws.
- `required` makes the initializer part of the mock's protocol conformance so subclasses of the (non-final) mock inherit it.
- When a protocol declares its own `init` requirements, the synthesized parameterless `init()` (normally generated for `public` / `package` mocks) is omitted.
- `resetMock()` clears `initCallCount` and `initCallArgs`.
- For `Sendable` and `actor` mocks the tracking is lock-backed like every other member (and `nonisolated` on actors); the `actor` witness omits `required`.

Supported for standalone protocols (including `Sendable` and `actor` mocks); inheriting protocols with `init` requirements emit a diagnostic.

### Protocol Inheritance

If a protocol inherits from another `@Mockable` protocol, the child mock inherits from the parent mock:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol Animal {
    func eat()
}

@Mockable
protocol Dog: Animal {
    func bark()
}

// DogMock inherits from AnimalMock
```

Child `resetMock()` calls `super.resetMock()` first.

For multiple parent protocols, the first parent is used as the mock superclass.

### Conditional Compilation

Protocol members inside `#if` / `#elseif` / `#else` are preserved in the generated mock. `resetMock()` includes matching conditional branches:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol PlatformService {
    func commonMethod()
    #if os(iOS)
    func iOSOnly()
    #else
    func nonIOSOnly()
    #endif
}
```

### `Sendable` Protocol Support

If a protocol inherits from `Sendable`, generated mocks conform to `@unchecked Sendable` and store mutable state behind `MockableLock` for thread safety:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol ThreadSafeService: Sendable {
    func process() async
}
// ThreadSafeServiceMock is @unchecked Sendable with lock-protected state
```

`MockableLock` uses `Mutex` (from `Synchronization`) on iOS 18.0+ / macOS 15.0+ / tvOS 18.0+ / watchOS 11.0+, and falls back to `NSLock`-based `LegacyLock` on older OS versions.

### `Actor` Protocol Support

If a protocol inherits from `Actor`, the generated mock is an actor. Helper members (call counters, argument collections, handlers, backing properties, `resetMock()`) are generated as `nonisolated` for test ergonomics:

```swift
@Mockable
protocol MyActorProtocol: Actor {
    func doWork() async
}
// MyActorProtocolMock is an actor with nonisolated test helpers
```

### `@MainActor` Protocol Support

Protocols annotated with `@MainActor` generate `@MainActor`-bound mocks.

## Behavioral Notes

- Return-value methods and get-only subscripts return a default when their handler is not set if the return type has one: Optionals return `nil`, arrays and sets return an empty collection, and dictionaries return an empty dictionary. Any other return type calls `fatalError`.
- Void-return methods and subscript setters are no-ops when handler is `nil`.
- `resetMock()` clears handlers, call counts, call arguments, and backing properties.
- For inherited protocols, `resetMock()` calls `super.resetMock()` before resetting child members.

## Diagnostics and Limitations

- `@Mockable` can only be applied to protocols.
- `@Mockable` does not accept arguments.
- Unsupported protocol members (e.g. a `static subscript`) emit compile-time diagnostics.
- `init` requirements are supported for standalone protocols (including `Sendable` and `actor` mocks); inheriting protocols with initializers emit a diagnostic (not yet supported).
- Static/class subscripts are not supported.
- For protocols with multiple parent protocols, the first parent is used as the mock superclass.

## Requirements

- Swift 5.9, 5.10, and 6.2+
- macOS 10.15+ / iOS 13+ / tvOS 13+ / watchOS 6+
- `MockableLock` lock strategy:
  - iOS 18.0+ / macOS 15.0+ / tvOS 18.0+ / watchOS 11.0+: uses `Mutex` (`Synchronization`)
  - Older OS versions: falls back to `LegacyLock` (`NSLock`-based)

## API Reference

### `@Mockable` Macro

```swift
@attached(peer, names: suffixed(Mock))
public macro Mockable()
```

Attach to a protocol to generate a `<ProtocolName>Mock` class.

### `MockableLock`

A thread-safe lock wrapper used internally by `Sendable` mock types. Uses `Mutex` when the `Synchronization` module is available (newer OS), otherwise falls back to `NSLock`-based locking.

## Links

- GitHub: https://github.com/yysskk/swift-mockable
- API Documentation: https://yysskk.github.io/swift-mockable/documentation/mockable
- License: MIT
